justification: Both terminology mismatches identified and mapped: session/token and suspended/locked. terminology_alignment section explicitly maps both pairs with definitions and origin attribution. Brief uses consistent terminology throughout with clear notes on which agent used which term. Both ACs preserved with exact error codes SESSION_EXPIRED and ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED. Architecture details include Redis TTL 1800, last_accessed_at field, and token issuance step. The suspended/locked mismatch is appropriately flagged as needing confirmation rather than silently resolved.

observations.text: terminology_alignment section maps both pairs explicitly with domain_term/technical_term structure and clear definitions
Correctly flags suspended/locked as 'ASSUMED to be the same concept' needing verification — does not silently resolve it
Both ACs preserve exact error codes: SESSION_EXPIRED (401) and ACCOUNT_SUSPENDED (403)
Architecture details complete: Redis TTL 1800s, last_accessed_at field, sliding window logic, token issuance guard
open_questions section elevates the suspended/locked risk as actionable — Shaq knows to block on this before implementing
Clear guidance on terminology usage: 'token' in code, 'session' in user-facing messages
Raises additional useful open questions (Redis eviction vs middleware, happy-path AC) without scope creep

agent_output_excerpt:
```yaml
handoff_brief:
  recipient: Shaq
  task_context: >
    Implement user authentication session management for an auth service. The feature
    covers two behaviors: (1) sliding session expiration — authenticated sessions expire
    after 30 minutes of inactivity and return 401 on stale requests; (2) suspended
    account enforcement — accounts in a blocked state are rejected at login with 403.
    Sessions are stored in Redis. The auth service owns token issuance and validation
    middlew
